nativeRange.habitat: Duckweed grows in freshwater ponds, lakes, ditches, and slow-moving streams, floating on the surface of still or sluggish nutrient-rich water across temperate and tropical climates worldwide, with the notable exception of arctic and subarctic regions.

avoidWith: tanks with submerged plants requiring high light

summary: Duckweed (Lemna minor) is one of the world's smallest flowering plants — tiny oval fronds just 1–8 mm across that carpet the water surface, absorbing excess nutrients and providing shade for fry and shrimp. Generally easy to grow, but INVASIVE: virtually impossible to fully remove once established.
careNotes: Duckweed (Lemna minor) is among the smallest flowering plants on Earth, made up of tiny 1–8 mm oval fronds, each trailing a single 1–2 cm root beneath the surface. It floats freely and reproduces almost exclusively by vegetative budding, with colonies capable of doubling in size every 2–3 days under good conditions, rapidly forming a dense green carpet. Duckweed generally tolerates a wide range of aquarium lighting, from dim ambient light to full-intensity setups. CO2 supplementation is not required, as the plant draws carbon dioxide directly from the air above the water. As a floating plant, it is a capable nutrient absorber, taking up ammonia, nitrate, and phosphate, which makes it a popular choice for shrimp tanks, fry-rearing setups, and as a natural supplement to filtration. Surface agitation must be kept low — strong filter output or aggressive surface skimmers will push fronds against the glass, where they accumulate and rot. CAUTION: Duckweed is widely considered invasive once introduced to an aquarium. Even a single surviving frond will re-establish a colony, and the plant spreads easily via splashes, nets, live plants, and even contact with wet fingertips. Once present, it is virtually impossible to fully remove. A dense surface mat also BLOCKS LIGHT to any plants growing below — making Duckweed incompatible with high-light planted tanks that contain carpeting plants or stem plants needing bright illumination. The most practical control method is manual skimming using a fine net or a small cup; goldfish and koi will also graze it readily. The species tolerates 6–33°C in the wild, with the recommended aquarium range being 15–30°C.
breedingNotes: Reproduces almost entirely by vegetative budding — each frond produces daughter fronds from lateral pouches, doubling the colony every 2–3 days under good light and nutrients. Flowering is extremely rare in cultivation and not relevant to propagation. To start a new tank, transfer even a single frond; growth will fill the surface within weeks.

primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales), native range, frond size (1–8 mm), water parameters (pH 6.5–8; temperature 6–33°C in wild), propagation, and invasiveness via bird dispersal.
